passages.explanation: Ananias and his wife Sapphira sell property like Barnabas did, but keep back part of the price while pretending to give the full amount. Peter confronts Ananias: no one forced you to sell, and the money was yours to keep. The lie wasn't to people but to the Holy Spirit — to God himself. Ananias drops dead on the spot. Young men wrap him up and carry him out for burial. Great fear seizes everyone who hears. This is a jarring moment — the generous community of chapter 4 has its first crisis of integrity.

About three hours later, Sapphira arrives not knowing what happened. Peter gives her a chance to tell the truth, asking if the amount they brought was the full sale price. She lies and confirms the false number. Peter asks how they could have agreed together to test the Spirit of the Lord — the men who buried her husband are at the door and will carry her out too. She falls dead immediately. Great fear comes over the whole church and everyone who hears. Luke uses the word 'church' (ekklesia) here for the first time in Acts.

Many signs and wonders are performed by the apostles. The believers meet together in Solomon's Porch. Outsiders hold them in high esteem but don't dare join casually — the Ananias and Sapphira incident has made the stakes clear. Yet believers keep being added, both men and women. People bring the sick into the streets hoping Peter's shadow might fall on them. Crowds also come from surrounding towns, bringing the sick and those tormented by unclean spirits, and all are healed.

The high priest and the Sadducees are filled with jealousy. They arrest the apostles and put them in public prison. But during the night, an angel opens the prison doors and tells them to go stand in the temple and speak all the words of this Life. They obey, entering the temple at daybreak and teaching. The contrast is stark — the authorities lock them up, God lets them out, and they go right back to what got them arrested.

The high priest convenes the council and sends for the prisoners, but the officers find the jail securely locked with guards at the doors — and no one inside. While they're confused by this, someone reports that the men they jailed are standing in the temple teaching. The officers bring them back without force, afraid the crowd might stone them. The high priest confronts them: we strictly ordered you not to teach in this name, and you've filled Jerusalem with your teaching and are trying to bring this man's blood on us.

Peter and the apostles give the same answer as before, even more directly: we must obey God rather than men. Then Peter preaches to the very council that condemned Jesus: the God of our fathers raised Jesus, whom you killed by hanging him on a tree. God exalted him to his right hand as Prince and Saviour, to give repentance and forgiveness to Israel. We are witnesses — and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God gives to those who obey him.

The council is furious and wants to kill them. But a Pharisee named Gamaliel — a respected teacher of the law (actually the apostle Paul's teacher) — stands up and orders the apostles removed briefly. He gives the council a pragmatic argument: other movements led by Theudas and Judas the Galilean attracted followers but collapsed when their leaders died. His advice: leave these men alone. If this movement is merely human, it will fail on its own. But if it's from God, you can't stop it — and you might find yourselves fighting against God.

The council is persuaded enough to follow Gamaliel's advice — partially. They call the apostles back, have them flogged, order them again not to speak in Jesus' name, and release them. The apostles leave rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer disgrace for the Name. Every day, in the temple and from house to house, they keep teaching and proclaiming Jesus as the Christ. The flogging doesn't slow them down at all.
